nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving shallow streams, flooded forest margins, and lagoons with dense leaf litter, submerged roots, and overhanging vegetation; water often stained dark brown with tannins

decorPreferences: coconut cave or ceramic cave for spawning site
dense planting (Amazon swords, Java fern, Vallisneria, floating plants for shade)
leaf litter (Indian almond/catappa leaves for tannins and shelter)
low ambient light or shaded areas

diet.notes: Primarily small live and frozen invertebrates: live or frozen brine shrimp (Artemia), daphnia, cyclops, micro-worms, and bloodworms (chironomid larvae). Will usually accept high-quality frozen and freeze-dried equivalents over time. High-quality micro-pellets and small cichlid pellets accepted by established fish. Variety is important for conditioning breeding pairs; live foods strongly recommended to trigger spawning.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetra, cardinal tetra, neon tetra)
small corydoras catfish (Corydoras habrosus, C. pygmaeus)
avoidWith: other Apistogramma species (territory and hybridisation risk)
boisterous fast-moving fish that stress the pair
large plecos that disrupt cave sites

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase reference photograph of male A. trifasciata showing the three lateral stripes and extended dorsal fin rays.

summary: A striking South American dwarf cichlid from the Paraguay basin and Pantanal; males display vivid blue flanks and dramatically elongated dorsal fin filaments. Cave-spawning with female-led brood care; naturally harem-polygynous. Comparatively tolerant of neutral, moderately hard water vs. other Apistogramma.
careNotes:
Apistogramma trifasciata is a member of the trifasciata species group and one of the more adaptable Apistogramma in terms of water chemistry, tolerating pH up to approximately 7.5 and moderate hardness (gH up to ~12 dH), making it accessible to hobbyists without RO water — though softer, slightly acidic conditions (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 2–8) are preferred for long-term health and breeding. Native to the Paraguay River drainage including the Pantanal wetlands of Brazil, Bolivia, and Paraguay, as well as the middle Amazon fringe; it inhabits shallow, slow-moving or still waters with leaf litter, roots, and heavy shading.

Males are noticeably larger than females and display vivid metallic blue-green flanks, a bold black lateral stripe, and characteristically long, filamentous extensions on the dorsal fin rays — this elongated dorsal distinguishes A. trifasciata from many congeners and can be used to identify males even at a distance. Females are smaller and yellower, turning bright lemon-yellow during brood care — a reliable indicator of active breeding.

A minimum 60 L tank with a fine sandy substrate, multiple caves (coconut shells, ceramic pots, inverted flowerpots), leaf litter, and dense planting provides the structure these fish need. Subdued lighting reduces stress. In harem setups (one male with two to three females), a larger tank of 100+ L with broken sightlines between female territories is recommended. Water changes of 20–30% weekly maintain quality; warm, stale water and elevated nitrates depress immune function and inhibit spawning. Temperature around 26–28°C is beneficial for conditioning. This species is sensitive to stale water but not as sensitive to low pH as strict blackwater Apistos.
breedingNotes: Spawns in caves; the female takes exclusive responsibility for egg guarding and fry tending while the male defends the outer territory perimeter. Clutch size is typically 50–120 eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 26°C; fry are free-swimming roughly 5–7 days later. The female may move the fry between pits in the substrate and will aggressively defend them against tankmates and even the male. First foods: freshly hatched Artemia nauplii, micro-worms, and infusoria; commercially available fry food as a supplement. The male can be separated after spawning if aggression toward the female is observed, though in spacious tanks with visual barriers the male typically does not disrupt the female's care. Soft, acidic water significantly improves fertilisation rates and fry survival; conditioning with live foods and a temperature increase of 1–2°C often triggers spawning.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma trifasciata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Paraguay, middle Amazon, Pantanal), water parameters (pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 18–179 ppm / ~1–10 dH, temperature 22–27°C), adult size (males to ~7 cm, females ~3.5 cm), diet (small invertebrates), tank setup, behaviour, and breeding notes. Cross-checked against FishBase and Aquarium Co-Op.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma trifasciata
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes), adult size (max ~7 cm TL for males), native range (Paraguay and lower Amazon basins, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, Argentina), temperature range (22–28°C), and diet (carnivore — small invertebrates). Confirmed trifasciata group membership.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma husbandry including harem setup guidance, cave requirements, diet (live/frozen invertebrates), water parameters, and recommended tank mates. Noted A. trifasciata as one of the hardier species tolerating slightly harder and more neutral water.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Apistogramma species guide
additional.notes: Confirmed species group placement (trifasciata group), elongated dorsal fin as a key male identification feature, female yellow colouration during brood care, female-led maternal mouthbrooding-adjacent cave care, and harem polygyny in the wild. Recommended sand substrate and leaf litter.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Apistogramma trifasciata species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source for breeding detail: clutch size 50–120 eggs, egg incubation ~48–72 hours at 26°C, fry free-swimming at day 5–7, female-exclusive brood care noted. Also confirmed male fin morphology (elongated dorsal fin filaments distinguishing trifasciata from other Apistogramma).

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner; female guards eggs and leads fry care exclusively while male defends territory perimeter. Clutch ~50–120 eggs; fry free-swimming at day 5–7. Soft, acidic water and live food conditioning recommended to trigger spawning. Male may be separated if he harasses the female post-spawn.
